mission.title: Install and Configure KubeRay on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeRay is a Kubernetes operator for running Ray clusters, jobs, and services. It manages the lifecycle of distributed Ray workloads for machine learning, hyperparameter tuning, reinforcement learning, and model serving. Installing the KubeRay operator lets platform teams run Ray on Kubernetes as first-class RayCluster, RayJob, and RayService resources.

steps.title: Add the KubeRay Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KubeRay Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kuberay https://ray-project.github.io/kuberay-helm/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the KubeRay operator
steps.description:
Install the operator into a new namespace called 'kuberay-operator'. The chart also installs the RayCluster, RayJob, and RayService CRDs.
```bash
helm install kuberay-operator kuberay/kuberay-operator --version 1.6.0 --namespace kuberay-operator --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the operator pod is running and the CRDs were installed.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kuberay-operator
kubectl get crd | grep ray.io
```

steps.title: Deploy a sample RayCluster
steps.description:
Use the companion ray-cluster chart to spin up a small cluster in the default namespace and confirm the operator reconciles it.
```bash
helm install raycluster kuberay/ray-cluster --version 1.6.0
kubectl get rayclusters
```

steps.title: Access the Ray dashboard
steps.description:
Port-forward the head service to reach the Ray dashboard from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/raycluster-kuberay-head-svc 8265:8265
```

uninstall.title: Remove the sample RayCluster
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the sample ray-cluster release first so the operator can clean up the underlying head and worker pods.
```bash
helm uninstall raycluster
```

uninstall.title: Remove the KubeRay operator
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the operator from the 'kuberay-operator' namespace.
```bash
helm uninstall kuberay-operator --namespace kuberay-operator
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Ray CRDs and the operator namespace to finish cleaning up. The chart installs four CRDs, and removing them deletes any remaining RayCluster, RayCronJob, RayJob, and RayService resources across the cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd rayclusters.ray.io raycronjobs.ray.io rayjobs.ray.io rayservices.ray.io
kubectl delete namespace kuberay-operator
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the Helm values for the operator before upgrading.
```bash
helm get values kuberay-operator --namespace kuberay-operator > kuberay-operator-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the KubeRay Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the KubeRay operator
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the next chart version. The chart version tracks the KubeRay release, so this is also an application upgrade.
```bash
helm upgrade kuberay-operator kuberay/kuberay-operator --namespace kuberay-operator --version 1.6.1
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the operator pod rolled out and is now running the new image.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kuberay-operator
kubectl get deployment kuberay-operator -n kuberay-operator -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}'
```

troubleshooting.title: Operator pod in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the operator pod will not start, the most common cause is conflicting CRDs from a previous install. Check the logs and the CRD state.
```bash
kubectl logs -n kuberay-operator deploy/kuberay-operator
kubectl get crd | grep ray.io
```

troubleshooting.title: RayCluster stuck with no head pod
troubleshooting.description:
If a RayCluster is created but no head pod appears, confirm the operator is watching events and check for resource quota or scheduling issues.
```bash
kubectl describe raycluster <name>
kubectl logs -n kuberay-operator deploy/kuberay-operator --tail=200
```

troubleshooting.title: Worker pods not scaling
troubleshooting.description:
If worker pods do not come up to match the RayCluster spec, check the resource requests against available node capacity.
```bash
kubectl get pods -l ray.io/cluster=<cluster-name>
kubectl describe pod <worker-pod-name>
```

troubleshooting.title: Dashboard not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the port-forward command succeeds but the dashboard does not load, the head pod may still be initializing the Ray processes. Check the head pod logs.
```bash
kubectl logs -l ray.io/node-type=head --tail=200
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeRay will show the operator pod running in 'kuberay-operator', the Ray CRDs registered on the cluster, and a sample RayCluster reconciled into a running head pod plus worker pods. The Ray dashboard is reachable through port forwarding. From here you can submit RayJob and RayService resources against the running cluster.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kuberay https://ray-project.github.io/kuberay-helm/
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine. For non-trivial workloads the cluster should have enough CPU and memory for the Ray head and worker pods.
